extra.reasoning:
The property requires that globally opted_out implies not sent, i.e., no reachable state has opted_out=true AND sent=true. The only transition that sets sent:=true while opted_out=true is [t_resume_glitch] (on send_attempt if opted_out and stage>=2). This is reachable because the optout event ([t_optout]) only sets opted_out:=true and sent:=false; it does NOT reset stage. So the lock can be advanced to stage 2 before opting out, leaving a 'resume glitch' that allows a send after opt-out.

Trace (initial: opted_out=F, sent=F, stage=0):
1. key_0 at stage 0 -> [adv_0]: stage:=1, sent:=F. (opted_out=F, sent=F, stage=1)
2. key_0 at stage 1 -> [adv_1]: stage:=2, sent:=F. (opted_out=F, sent=F, stage=2)
3. optout -> [t_optout]: opted_out:=T, sent:=F; stage stays 2. (opted_out=T, sent=F, stage=2)
4. send_attempt: [t_send_allowed] disabled (opted_out true); [t_send_blocked] disabled (stage<2 false, stage==2); [t_resume_glitch] enabled (opted_out and stage>=2) -> sent:=T. Resulting state: opted_out=T, sent=T, stage=2.

This state has opted_out=true and sent=true, violating globally(opted_out implies not sent). The counterexample uses 4 events, within the 5-event bound.
